source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.6.7-9.6.8 nice/renice/top (pp. 166-167)

questions.q: Launch 'find / -name "file"' with a nice value of 5, using the guide's recommended form.
questions.why: nice -n <value> <command> starts the command at that niceness; 'nice -n N' is the recommended, portable form.

questions.q: If you run 'nice' with a command but NO value option, what nice value is applied?
questions.why: With no option, nice sets the value to 10; running 'nice' alone just prints the current shell's nice value.

questions.q: What are an ordinary (non-root) user's limits when setting niceness?
options: They can only REDUCE priority - only values 0 to 19 are accepted
They can set any value from -20 to 19
They cannot use nice at all
questions.why: A standard user can only lower a process's priority (higher nice), accepting 0-19; negative values need root.

questions.q: Change the priority of the running process with PID 1664 to nice value 15.
questions.why: renice changes the priority of an EXISTING process; -p selects it by PID.

questions.q: What is the difference between nice and renice?
options: nice sets priority when LAUNCHING a command; renice changes an already-RUNNING process
nice is for running processes; renice is for new ones
nice is root-only; renice is not
questions.why: nice applies at launch time; renice adjusts existing processes (and can target by -p PID, -u UID or -g GID).

questions.q: What does the top command provide?
options: A real-time, interactive display of processes and their CPU/memory consumption
A list of background jobs in the current shell
questions.why: top shows processes and resource usage live and interactively.

questions.q: In top's columns, what does NI show?
options: The nice value of the process
questions.why: NI is the nice value; PR is the priority, %CPU and %MEM the resource loads.
